1. Introduction to Bilingual Education.desc:
- This unit deals with [bilingual education] in general.

- The unit focuses on [definition of bilingual education], [types and models of bilingual education].

- It also looks at the [benefits of bilingual education] and the [challenges associated with bilingual education].
ex: Unit 3, Session 2 focuses on bilingual education.
Topics include: definition, types, models, benefits, and challenges of bilingual education.

2. Definition of Bilingual Education.desc:
- Many people are conversant with the principles of bilingual education but lack the concept of bilingual education (Baker 2001).

- [Bilingual education] is a complex phenomenon and distinction should be made with regard to how it is used and the promotion of languages and the teaching of children whose languages are different from the school language.

- The situation in Ghana relates to the different languages found across the various ethnic lines.

- Several attempts have been made by many authors towards the meaning of bilingual education:

  - Bilingual education is the situation whereby [two languages are used within the same school] (Edwards, 1984 cited in Baker 1988).

  - Bilingual education is the use of [two languages in education at a particular time], at varying amounts of time or used concurrently (Hamer and Blanco, 1989).

  - Mora (2000) sees bilingual education as the use of both [English (L2) and a child's L1 as medium of instruction] at different points and in a program for different functions and purposes.

  - Bilingual education is the use of [two languages as the medium of instruction] (US Office of Bilingual education and Minority Languages affairs, 2000).

- Based on the above definitions, bilingual education involves:
  - [Two languages used] in the educational system
  - A [varying amount of time] used for each language
  - Used [sequentially or concurrently]
ex: Bilingual education involves using two languages in the educational system.
Edwards (1984): two languages used within the same school.
Hamer and Blanco (1989): two languages used at varying amounts of time, sequentially or concurrently.
Mora (2000): use of both English (L2) and child's L1 as medium of instruction for different purposes.
US Office (2000): two languages used as medium of instruction.
Key elements: two languages, varying time allocation, sequential or concurrent use.

3. Types/Models of Bilingual Education.desc:
- There are various forms of bilingual education. This has occurred as a result of the inability to draw a clear distinction between [bilingual education] and [English as a second language (ESL)].

- ESL and EFL (English as a Foreign Language) programs are geared towards [second language learning] while bilingual education is about the [use of language as a medium of instruction].

- Classification into the following types:

  ### <u>Additive Bilingualism</u>
  - This is where students [maintain their minority language]. Students do not lose their L1 in the process of learning the L2.

  ### <u>Subtractive Bilingualism</u>
  - This is where students [lose their minority language or L1] in the process of acquiring the L2.

  ### <u>Transitional Bilingualism</u>
  - This is where the child shifts from his or her minority language (L1) to the majority language (L2). This is done to assimilate them socially and culturally into the majority language.
  - Two sub-divisions:
    - [Early exit transitional bilingual education]: allows for two years or three years of use of the minority language (L1).
    - [Late exit transitional bilingual education]: allows for around 40% of teaching in the mother tongue until the sixth year (Baker 2001).
    - The early-exit transitional bilingual education sometimes lasts for three years in Ghana.

  ### <u>Maintenance Bilingual Education</u>
  - Fosters the [minority language in the child]. It strengthens the child's sense of cultural identity and affirms the rights of ethnic minority children (Baker 2001).
  - Maintenance bilingualism programs are [pluralistic] and promote bilingualism and literacy for language minority students (Lessow-Hurley 1996).
  - Maintenance bilingual education is [additive] and associated with positive cognitive benefit (Cummins 1981).
  - Sub-divided into:
    - [Static maintenance]: maintains the language skills at the level at which the child enters school.
    - [Developmental maintenance]: develops the child's L1 to full proficiency (Baker 2001). Also known as [Enrichment Bilingual education] for language minority children.

  ### <u>Submersion Bilingual Education</u>
  - This is a program where minority language students are [prevented from using their home language] while the curriculum is taught in the L2 (Baker 1988).
  - The aim is [assimilative] and the outcome is to make learners monolinguals in the minority language (Baker 2001).
  - The first language of the child is lost, so the model is considered [subtractive bilingualism]. This leads to negative cognitive effects and school dropout (Cummins 1981).

  ### <u>Immersion Bilingual Education</u>
  - It comprises [only minority students]. The teacher uses a simplified version of the majority language and may initially accept children or learners contributing in the L1.
  - Its goal is ensuring [proficiency in the minority language] (such as Twi) for language majority (English) students.
  - It is [assimilative] and the outcome is [additive bilingualism] (Lessow-Hurley 1996).
  - Variation may depend on the age at which a child commences:
    - [Early immersion]
    - [Middle immersion]
    - [Late immersion]
  - However, immersion bilingual education can be [pluralistic or assimilative] depending on who is immersed (Baker, 2001).
ex: Additive bilingualism: students maintain their minority L1 while learning L2.
Subtractive bilingualism: students lose their L1 in the process of acquiring L2.
Transitional bilingualism: child shifts from L1 to L2 for assimilation.
Early exit transitional: L1 used for 2-3 years.
Late exit transitional: L1 used for up to 40% of teaching until 6th year.
Maintenance bilingual education: fosters minority language and cultural identity.
Maintenance is additive and has positive cognitive benefits.
Static maintenance: maintains L1 at entry level.
Developmental maintenance: develops L1 to full proficiency (enrichment).
Submersion: minority students prevented from using L1; taught only in L2.
Submersion is subtractive, assimilative, leads to negative cognitive effects.
Immersion: minority students taught in simplified L2, may use L1 initially.
Immersion goals: proficiency in minority language for majority students.
Immersion types: early, middle, late based on starting age.
Immersion can be pluralistic or assimilative depending on context.

4. Benefits of Bilingual Education.desc:
- Bilingual education was seen as having a negative effect on children's learning from the 1920's to the 1960's, but current studies have shown that bilingual education is [useful to the child].

- Bilingual education has been found to be [cognitively, linguistically, academically, and socially beneficial] to the child (Baker, 2001).

  ### <u>Bilingual Education and Cognitive Benefits</u>
  - [Cognitive flexibility]: makes learners intellectually flexible. Bilinguals have a more flexible view of the world and are more versatile in processing information (Lemon & Goggin, 1989).
  - [Linguistic and metalinguistic abilities]: bilingual children are able to separate word sound from word meaning, detecting the loose connection between phonetic word and its meaning (Leopold, 1961). Bilingual learners perform better on naming and sentence construction tasks than monolinguals. They perform better on both verbal and non-verbal tests of intelligence (Weatherford, 1986).
  - [Concept formation]: bilinguals perform better on tests of linear measurement than monolinguals. They have the ability to conserve and measure length and grasp the concept of linear measurement (Liedtke & Nelson, 1968) due to diverse linguistic and cultural experience.
  - [Divergent thinking skills and creativity]: bilinguals are more creative and deal with complex problems more effectively than monolinguals. They are more divergent in their thinking (Bamford & Mizokawa, 1991).
  - [Cognitive style]: different ways one perceives, remembers, sorts, performs, and utilizes information (Ronstadt, 1996). Bilinguals are analytical, articulate, and able to draw meaningful conclusions (Diaz, 1983). Bilinguals do better in mental organization and abstract things; they see things in a flexible and versatile way when processing information.

  ### <u>Academic Benefits of Bilingual Education</u>
  - Learners remain [higher academic achievers] than monolinguals throughout their schooling (Thomas, Collier and Abbott, 1993).
  - Students who take part in bilingual education consistently have [high scores in reading, language skills, and Mathematics] (Willig, 1985).
  - Bialystok (1997) observed that bilingual children [understood what they read better] than monolingual children.
  - Bilingual learners [perform better as they progress] than monolingual learners (Greenre, 1998).

  ### <u>Benefits of Bilingual Education to the Learner</u>
  - Individual in bilingual education has a [cognitive advantage] over monolingual counterparts, leading to better performance in school and job market (Hakuta, 1986).
  - Bilingual individuals are able to [communicate with family members] and with other members of their native language, helping them have a healthy sense of multiculturalism (Wong-Fillmore, 1991; Cho & Tse, 1997; Krashen, 1998).
  - Bilingual education makes the child [accept people of other cultures] and able to resolve cultural conflicts (Tse, 1998).

  ### <u>Benefits of Bilingual Education to the Society</u>
  - People fluent in more than one language can enhance their countries' [economic competence abroad] to maintain potential and security interest (Marcos, 1999 cited in Marcos, 2001).
  - [Media correspondents, teachers, health workers, law enforcement, lawyers, civil servants, agriculture extension officers, airline employees] could perform effectively if bilingual, as they can interact effectively with people of different languages and cultures.
  - Society benefits in terms of [business diplomacy and national security] (Fishman, 1990).
ex: Bilingual education has cognitive, linguistic, academic, and social benefits.
Cognitive flexibility: bilinguals process information more versatilely.
Linguistic/metalinguistic: better at separating sound from meaning.
Concept formation: better at linear measurement tasks.
Divergent thinking: more creative and effective with complex problems.
Cognitive style: more analytical, articulate, and flexible.
Academic: higher achievement throughout schooling.
Higher scores in reading, language, and mathematics.
Better reading comprehension and progress over time.
Learner benefits: cognitive advantage in school and job market.
Communication with family and multicultural awareness.
Acceptance of other cultures and resolution of cultural conflicts.
Societal benefits: economic competence, national security, business diplomacy.
Professionals perform better with bilingual skills.

5. Criticisms of Bilingual Education.desc:
- Bilingual education has been criticized by people. There have always been divided opinions among politicians, educationists, bilingual researchers, and the general public on whether bilingual education is necessary.

- Some criticisms:
  - It is said that bilingual education is a [hindrance to learning in English] and detrimental to children's short-term and long-term academic achievement.
  - Bambose argues that many languages in a society [impede national integration] and that differences between indigenous languages keep the people apart.
  - Crawford (1998) sees bilingual education as [costly and a drain on government finances].
ex: Critics say bilingual education hinders English learning and academic achievement.
Bambose: many languages impede national integration.
Crawford: bilingual education is costly and drains government finances.

6. Summary of Session Two.desc: - In this session, we have been looking at some [definitions of bilingual education], [bilingual education in Ghana], [types of bilingual education], [benefits of bilingual education], and we ended with some [criticisms of bilingual education].
ex: Session covered definitions of bilingual education.
Types and models of bilingual education were discussed.
Benefits of bilingual education were examined.
Criticisms of bilingual education were presented.

1. Introduction to the Language Situatio.desc:
- This session takes a closer look at the [language situation of Ghana].

- The number of languages in the country will be discussed.

- It will take a closer look at the [types of language groups] in Ghana and why there is controversy about the exact number of languages in Ghana.
ex: Session focuses on the language situation in Ghana.
Topics include: number of languages, language groups, and controversy over exact count.

2. Languages of Ghana.desc:
- Ghana has a total landmass of about [238,305 kilometers] (92,660 sq. miles) and has a population of [24 million] (World FactBook, 2010), estimated at [28.83 million] in 2017 (Ghana Statistical Service, 2017).

- Ghana is made up of numerous and divergent [ethnic groups and languages].

- Over [forty (40) languages] are mutually unintelligible.

- The precise number of languages spoken within the borders of Ghana is [not known yet]. There is controversy among writers over the definite number.

- Estimates made by authors:
  - Denny (1963): between [47 and 62] languages identifiable in Ghana.
  - Boadi (1971) and Andoh-Kumi (1994): [40] languages.
  - Hall (1983): [44] mutually unintelligible languages.
  - Dolphyne and Dakubu (1988): between [45 and 50] languages.
  - Melchers (1992): [42] languages in Ghana.
  - Bambgose (2000): [57] mutually unintelligible speech forms.

- The local languages in Ghana are grouped under [Niger-Congo language family] and further grouped into:
  - [Kwa languages] (Southern languages)
  - [Gur languages] (Northern languages) (Andoh-Kumi, 1994).

- Other languages added to the local languages:
  - [English] (unofficial language of Ghana)
  - [Hausa] (Chadic-Afro-Asiatic), spoken widely in the Northern part of the country
  - Two types of [Mande language group] (Lighi and Bisa) are found in Ghana.

- The above situation creates a [complex picture] of the language situation in Ghana.
ex: Ghana's population: 24 million (2010), estimated 28.83 million (2017).
Over 40 languages are mutually unintelligible.
Exact number of languages is controversial and unknown.
Denny (1963): 47-62 languages.
Boadi (1971), Andoh-Kumi (1994): 40 languages.
Hall (1983): 44 mutually unintelligible languages.
Dolphyne and Dakubu (1988): 45-50 languages.
Melchers (1992): 42 languages.
Bambgose (2000): 57 mutually unintelligible speech forms.
Languages belong to Niger-Congo family.
Two main groups: Kwa (Southern) and Gur (Northern).
Other languages: English, Hausa, Mande (Lighi and Bisa).
The language situation in Ghana is complex.

3. Why Differences Exist in Exact Number.desc:
- It is difficult to [distinguish between languages and dialect]. For instance, some authors see Akan as one language with three major dialects (Fante, Asante-Twi, and Akuapem-Twi), but others see them as separate languages because they have their own literary materials (Obeng-Gyasi, 1997).

- Lack of [clear-cut ethnic and linguistic identities]: Ethnic groups overlap and are easily modified in areas of high cultural convergence because of colonial administration.

- Lack of [limited and outdated data] on many Ghanaian languages, especially those in major areas (Andoh-Kumi, 1994). This results in varying number of names given to languages, which makes it confusing.

- [Attitude of people] is also a factor: Speakers of minority languages may want to identify themselves with other larger and prestigious groups, while others consider their languages or dialects to be superior and would not want to have anything to do with another dialect or language, though they are mutually intelligible (Dolphyne & Dakubu, 1988).
ex: Difficulty distinguishing between languages and dialects causes variation in counts.
Akan example: some consider it one language with dialects; others consider dialects separate languages due to literary materials.
Lack of clear ethnic and linguistic identities due to colonial administration and cultural convergence.
Limited and outdated data on many Ghanaian languages.
Attitudes: minority speakers identify with larger groups; some consider their language superior and reject mutually intelligible varieties.

4. Summary of Session Three.desc: - So far, we have looked at the [language situation of Ghana] and the difficulties which have made it difficult to identify the exact number of languages in the country.
ex: Session covered the language situation in Ghana.
Difficulties in determining exact number of languages were discussed.

1. Introduction to Language Policy.desc:
- Decisions on the [status of a language] are policy decisions, while most corpus activities are in the realm of implementation.

- This unit discusses the [types of language policy] and [problems associated with the implementation of language policy].
ex: Status decisions are policy decisions; corpus activities relate to implementation.
Session covers types of language policy and implementation challenges.

2. Definitions of Language Policy.desc:
- [Language policy] is a program of action on the role or status of a language in a given community (Bambose, 1990, p.1).

- Language policy is what governments officially do through [legislation, executive actions, or other means] to determine how languages are to be used in public contexts, cultivate language skills needed to meet national priorities, or establish rights of individuals or groups to learn, use, and maintain languages (Schiffman, 1999).
ex: Bambose (1990): Language policy is a program of action on language role or status.
Schiffman (1999): Language policy involves government actions determining language use, cultivating skills, and establishing language rights.

3. Types of Language Policy.desc:
- Noss (1971, p. 1) identifies [three types of language policy]:

  ### <u>Official Language Policy</u>
  - It is the [recognition by the government] and the determination of the purposes for which languages have been recognized.

  ### <u>Educational Language Policy</u>
  - It involves [recognition given to some languages] by the government and educational authorities and agencies for use as [medium of instruction in schools].

  ### <u>General Language Policy</u>
  - It is the [unofficial recognition] given to languages that are used in [mass media, business, and contacts with foreigners].
ex: Official language policy: government recognition and determination of language purposes.
Educational language policy: recognition of languages for use as medium of instruction in schools.
General language policy: unofficial recognition for languages used in mass media, business, and foreign contacts.

4. Aspects of Language Policy.desc:
- Language policy can be [explicit or implicit] (Andoh-Kumi, 1974).

- [Explicit language policy] is stated clearly in a document.

- [Implicit language policy] is inferred from the practices of the people or governmental bodies or other agencies (without any official backing).
ex: Explicit policy: clearly stated in a document.
Implicit policy: inferred from practices without official backing.

5. Problems of Language Policy Implement.desc:
- Eastman (1983, p. 83) claims that one of the more common language policy problems is [deciding what language should be the medium of instruction].

- Challenges confronting language planning and policy implementation in Ghana:

  - [Frequent changes in government]: The inability to use a local language as a medium of instruction has occurred because of frequent changes in government. Each change in government meant a change in policy and a subsequent change in teacher training and teaching materials (Bambgose, 1991).

  - [Implementation procedure not specified]: Implementation procedures of language policies in Africa are left unspecified in the policy statements. Bambgose (1990) claims that a policy formulation that does not state whose responsibility it is to enforce the policy and provide adequate resources to back such implementation amounts to carrying on with no existing policy.

  - [Negative attitudes of officials and some parents]: Some educated Africans think that any education acquired through the L1 is an inferior one. Most elites in Ghana have the perception that [real education can only be acquired in a world language like French or English].

  - [Lack of Enforcement of the policy]: Most governments think that enforcing a language policy may lead to tribalism or racism. In Ghana, the government and Ghana Education Service do not enforce the use of L1 as the medium of instruction in schools at the early grade.

  - [The expensive nature of the policy in multilingual societies]: Implementing a language policy in a multilingual environment involves a lot of cost. The cost involved in producing teacher learner resources for the various local languages is very expensive and places a huge financial burden on the country.

  - [The multilingual nature of some countries]: Most classrooms in Ghana are multilingual and make it impossible to use the various languages as the mediums of instruction. Many children would have to learn one of the eleven languages studied in school to follow lessons in the first three years (Andoh-Kumi, 1994). This places some pupils at a disadvantage because they will have to learn a new language.
ex: Eastman: Common problem is deciding medium of instruction.
Frequent government changes lead to policy changes affecting teacher training and materials.
Implementation procedures often unspecified, lacking responsibility and resources.
Negative attitudes: some see L1 education as inferior; elites prefer world languages.
Lack of enforcement due to fear of tribalism or racism.
Expensive to produce resources for multiple local languages.
Multilingual classrooms make implementation difficult; some pupils must learn new language to follow lessons.

6. Ways of Addressing the Problems of La.desc:
- Suggestions to address challenges towards implementation of language policy:

  - There should be [effective advocacy policy] to send information on the language policy of education in Ghana, the benefits of starting the child's education using the L1, and studying the L1 in schools.

  - There is the need to [study the local languages and culture] in schools.

  - There should be [provision of adequate resources] to ensure compliance of the language policy of education.

  - There should be [effective monitoring and evaluation] of policy implementation by GES, Ministry of Education, and concerned stakeholders.

  - There should be [incentives for compliance and deterrent measures for noncompliance] of the language policy in education in schools.

  - There is the need for [government's support and the will] to implement the language policy of education in Ghana.

  - There is the need to [produce textbooks in the Ghanaian language] to support the implementation of the language policy.

  - There should be [change of negative attitudes] towards the use of L1 as medium of instruction in schools, especially at the Early Grade and Basic levels.

  - There should be [adequate training of effective and efficient language teachers] to ensure proper implementation.

  - There is the need to do a [regular socio-linguistic study] of early grade classrooms to deal with the multilingual nature in the country.

  - The posting of early grade teachers should take into consideration the [L1 and L2 of those teachers] to ensure pupil-teacher language match.
ex: Effective advocacy to inform about L1 benefits in education.
Study local languages and culture in schools.
Provide adequate resources for compliance.
Effective monitoring and evaluation by GES and Ministry.
Incentives for compliance; deterrents for noncompliance.
Government support and political will essential.
Produce textbooks in Ghanaian languages.
Change negative attitudes toward L1 instruction.
Train effective and efficient language teachers.
Regular socio-linguistic studies of early grade classrooms.
Post teachers considering their L1 and L2 for language match with pupils.

7. Summary of Session Four.desc: - In this session, we have discussed the [concept of language planning and language policy], the [policy in education], the [challenges of the language policy implementation in Ghana], and [ways to address those challenges].
ex: Session covered concept of language planning and policy.
Educational language policy was discussed.
Challenges of language policy implementation in Ghana were examined.
Solutions to address implementation challenges were presented.

1. Introduction to Misconceptions.desc:
- Dear student, you are welcome to another session of unit 4. Here, we shall be focusing on some [misconceptions against the use of L1] as a medium of instruction in our schools.

- Try to remember some of these misconceptions as they will help you to understand this part better.
ex: Session focuses on misconceptions against using L1 as medium of instruction.
Understanding misconceptions helps in better comprehension of the topic.

2. Definition of Misconception.desc: - [Misconception] can be defined as the wrong perception people have about the learning of the Mother tongue (L1).
ex: Misconception: wrong perception about learning of mother tongue (L1).

3. Misconceptions Against the Use of L1.desc:
- It is argued that the L1 [cannot be used to express modern concepts] in education and a combination of both L1 and L2 [create confusion] for the child and the L1 should be [pushed aside] for L2. These arguments are not supported by empirical research.

- It is argued that both parents and teachers want their children to be educated well in the [L2] which is supposedly argued to be the best language of education. However, research has shown that when parents are educated on the benefits of L1 use in education and presented with appropriate options to select from, they overwhelmingly [opted for L1 and L2] instead of only L2 (Andoh-Kumi, 2001; CAL, 2001).

- It is argued that the [multilingual nature of Ghana and its classrooms] does not allow the use of L1 as a medium of instruction. We need to take a [sociolinguistic mapping] of our lower primary classroom and see which languages are represented in which classroom and in which district.

- It is again argued that when teachers are posted to their language area or locality to teach at the lower level, it will bring about [divisiveness in the country] which will work against the vision of integrating the ethnic groups in Ghana. This assertion is interesting. Then, parents should be prevented from taking care of their children but be sent to people in another ethnic group to nurture them. Children need a [familiar person who speaks their language] at the early stages of education to be able to adjust to the schooling system.

- There is the myth that [foreign language (L2) is often seen as necessary] for further studies, work, and other opportunities, yet as Phillipson (1992) points out, this has not happened in a political vacuum but is the result of [deliberate promotion by powerful countries] of their respective languages.

- There is the argument that [bilingualism causes confusion] and that the first language must be pushed aside so that the second language can be learned. Research has proven that this assertion is false since the [more highly developed the first language skills, the better the results in the second language] because language and cognition in the second language builds on the first language (Cummins 1997, 2000; Ramirez et al, 1991; Thomas & Collier 2002).
ex: Misconception 1: L1 cannot express modern concepts; L1 and L2 cause confusion.
Misconception 2: Parents and teachers prefer L2 as best medium of instruction.
Research shows parents opt for both L1 and L2 when informed of benefits.
Misconception 3: Multilingual nature of Ghana prevents L1 instruction.
Need for sociolinguistic mapping of lower primary classrooms.
Misconception 4: Posting teachers to language areas causes divisiveness.
Children need familiar person speaking their language for adjustment.
Misconception 5: Foreign language (L2) is necessary for opportunities.
Phillipson: This is result of deliberate promotion by powerful countries.
Misconception 6: Bilingualism causes confusion; L1 must be pushed aside.
Research proves: stronger L1 skills lead to better L2 results.
L2 builds on L1 language and cognition.

4. Research-Based Counterarguments.desc:
- Cummins (2000) and Thomas and Collier (2002) argue that the [more highly developed the L1 skills of the child, the better their results in the L2].

- Research has shown that when parents are educated on the benefits of L1 use in education and presented with appropriate options to select from, they overwhelmingly [opted for L1 and L2] instead of only L2 (Andoh-Kumi, 2001; CAL, 2001).

- We need to take a [sociolinguistic mapping] of our lower primary classroom and see which languages are represented in which classroom and in which district.

- Children need a [familiar person who speaks their language] at the early stages of education to be able to adjust to the schooling system.

- The myth that foreign language is necessary for opportunities is the result of [deliberate promotion by powerful countries] of their respective languages (Phillipson, 1992).

- Research has proven that the argument that bilingualism causes confusion is false since the [more highly developed the first language skills, the better the results in the second language] because language and cognition in the second language builds on the first language (Cummins 1997, 2000; Ramirez et al, 1991; Thomas & Collier 2002).
ex: Cummins, Thomas & Collier: Highly developed L1 leads to better L2 results.
Informed parents choose both L1 and L2, not only L2.
Sociolinguistic mapping needed to understand classroom language composition.
Familiar language speaker helps child adjust to school system.
L2 dominance results from deliberate promotion by powerful countries.
Bilingualism does not cause confusion; L1 development supports L2 learning.

5. Summary of Session Five.desc:
- In this session, we have discussed the [misconceptions of using the L1 as a medium of instruction] in our schools in the country.

- We hope that you will do a further debate of this important topic during your group meetings.
ex: Session covered misconceptions against using L1 as medium of instruction.
Further debate on this topic is encouraged during group meetings.
